1: [warmly] Welcome. Make yourselves comfortable. You don't need to remember all of this… [softly] you just need to feel a little more at home in what lies ahead. [reassuring] It's completely natural to feel both excited and a little nervous right now. This is a calm moment together, entirely at your own pace.
2: [warmly] We'll take this in a gentle order. First, how you'll notice it's time, then the phases of labour, one at a time, so you'll recognise them when they come. [pause] We'll look at the room and who's there, go through pain relief and breathing, and how the partner can be a real source of support. You don't need to cram this like it's an exam… [softly] a lot of it settles on its own just by hearing it. And interrupt and ask whenever you like, [warmly] that's exactly why we're here.
3: [warmly] Many people wonder beforehand: how will I really know it's time? [pause] And almost everyone recognises it when it actually happens. The body tends to give signs: contractions that come closer together, last longer, and grow stronger, the waters breaking, or the mucus plug coming away. [softly] This thing with early, practice contractions is tricky, because they can feel sharp but they're irregular and uneven in length, and they often settle if you rest or change position. Real, active contractions carry on no matter what you do, they don't care about a warm bath or a nap. [pause] A rule of thumb for when it's time to head in: the contractions come about every four minutes, last around one minute, and have been doing so for an hour. [reassuring] But trust your gut too… if the waters break, or you feel worried, then you go in, and it's always right to call first.
4: [warmly] It can feel reassuring to have a map of what's going to happen, so let's draw it out. A birth is usually divided into three stages. [pause] First, the dilation stage, the longest one, when the cervix softens and opens all the way to ten centimetres. That's the body's great work, and it's allowed to take its time. Then the pushing stage, when you bear down and the baby is born. And finally the placental stage, when the placenta comes, a little while after the baby. [softly] Good to know: this almost never goes in a straight line. It can come in waves, slow down and pick up again, [reassuring] and that doesn't mean anything is wrong… it's just how bodies work.
5: [warmly] Many people picture the delivery room as something clinical and cold, but it's usually calmer than you'd think, and for the time you're there, it's your room. [pause] The bed can be adjusted into different positions, and a monitor listens now and then to the baby's heartbeat, so you know everything is fine. Many rooms have a birthing ball, a shower or a bath. The lighting can be dimmed, and there's always space for the partner. [softly] This is worth taking in: you really do get to influence the light, the music, and who's in the room. Say what you'd like, it isn't being a bother… [reassuring] it helps us make it good for you.
6: [warmly] It's comforting to know who you'll meet. In the room there's always a midwife, who leads the birth and receives the baby, and an assistant nurse who helps out and is there for you, both practically and as a calming presence. [pause] If needed, a doctor will come in, a paediatrician for the baby or an anaesthetist if something like an epidural becomes relevant. [softly] One thing that often surprises people: the midwife isn't in the room the whole time, she comes and goes. That's completely normal and means everything is rolling along as it should. [reassuring] You're never alone with the responsibility, there's always a button to press… but you're not being watched every second either, you get to just be the two of you.
7: [warmly] Now let's go into the phases. The latent phase is the warm-up. The cervix softens and opens to about three or four centimetres, and the contractions are still a little irregular. [pause] This is the phase that can test your patience, because it can go on for hours, sometimes up to a whole day the first time you give birth. [softly] Try not to stay awake counting every contraction here… the most important advice I can give is to carry on as normal for as long as you can. Stay home as long as it feels safe, eat lightly, drink often, take a walk or a shower, and rest or sleep if you can. Save your strength. [reassuring] And partner, your role now is to create calm, keep a quiet eye on the timings in the background, and make sure the mother gets fluids and a little food without making a big thing of it.
8: [warmly] In the active phase the body shifts up a gear. The contractions become regular, strong and closer together, and the opening goes from about four to ten centimetres. Now you're usually with the midwife. [pause] A contraction builds like a wave… it rises, reaches a peak, and then lets go. And between the waves there's a pause, and that pause is your rest, [softly] really take it. Mother, find your rhythm with breathing and movement, and take one contraction at a time, you don't need to think about the next one. [reassuring] Partner, stay close now: breathe with her, give water between contractions, press firmly against the small of her back, and be her voice to the staff so she doesn't have to speak herself.
9: [warmly] The transition phase is the short, intense bit right before the pushing. [pause] This is often the hardest moment, and this is where the doubt comes: the feeling of not being able to go on, maybe even wanting to go home or be somewhere else. [reassuring] I want you to hear this now, calmly and clearly: that doubt is actually a good sign. It usually means it's about to turn, that you're nearly there. [softly] Time can also feel completely different here, a short moment can feel endless. Partner, this is the moment not to back away. Stay right there, a calm voice, eye contact, and remind her that she's already doing this, she's right in the middle of it.
10: [warmly] Then something new comes: a strong, almost irresistible urge to push, the body wants to bear down. [pause] You don't need to know how to do this in advance, you follow your body and the midwife's guidance, pushing with the contraction and relaxing in between. It can take anything from a few minutes to a good hour, and both are completely normal. [softly] There's a burning and stretching for a while as the baby comes, and that's a sign that you're really close. [reassuring] Partner, support her upper body, hold her hand, breathe along. Soon, very soon, you'll meet your baby.
11: [warmly] And then they're here. Right after the birth the baby is placed skin to skin on the mother's chest, for warmth, for security, and often for the first breastfeed, entirely at the baby's own pace. [pause] The placenta is delivered with a gentle contraction a little while later, and it's usually something you barely notice amid everything else. [softly] Partner, you're welcome to cut the umbilical cord if you'd like, and have some skin to skin too, it's lovely for the baby to feel you both. [reassuring] And then you get to be just that… the three of you, a little while in your own bubble.
12: [warmly] Prophylaxis sounds advanced but it's really just simple tools you practise in advance, so they're almost there on their own when it counts. [pause] The foundation is the breathing: breathe in calmly through the nose, and longer out through the mouth. A whole contraction is really just a few deep breaths, and then it's over. [softly] At the same time, let go of the tension where we tend to hold it, in the shoulders, the jaw and the hands, because a relaxed body simply hurts less. And let your gaze rest on a single point, or on the partner's voice. [reassuring] My best tip: practise together a few times at home, calmly, so the calm sits in the body and not just in the head when you actually need it.
13: [warmly] There's actually a great deal of pain relief with no medication at all, and it's often underestimated. [pause] Moving and changing position helps the body and gives you something to do. Warmth feels good, from a shower, a wheat bag or a bath. Massage and firm pressure against the small of the back, the breathing you've practised, a TENS machine, and sterile water injections for back pain specifically. [softly] And one thing that sounds gentle but really does matter: a calm environment, dimmed light and a sense of safety actually lower the experience of pain itself. [reassuring] Fear tightens the body, and calm opens it.
14: [warmly] And then there's medication, because it should be there for you. [pause] Laughing gas, which you breathe during the contraction, takes the edge off the peak and you stay clear-headed. The epidural, the EDA, is the most powerful and is given by an anaesthetist. There's also a pudendal block late in the process, and morphine-related options that are sometimes given early so you can rest. [reassuring] The most important thing I want to say is this: none of it is cheating, and there's no prize for enduring. [softly] The right pain relief is simply the one that helps you, and you're allowed to change your mind along the way, as many times as you like.
15: [warmly] Affirmations are little words to hold on to when it gets heavy. Some you say to yourself, and some the partner can say to you. [softly] Press the play buttons, and you'll hear how they can sound… and feel for which ones become yours.
16: [warmly] If you, as the partner, wonder where you should be, the answer is almost always: by her face, at eye level. [pause] That's where you do the most good, for the breathing, the eye contact and your calm voice, you become her anchor. In standing positions you can stand beside or behind her and press against the small of her back, and during the pushing you're up by her upper body. [softly] Stay out of the midwife's way, and you don't have to look down if you don't want to, that's completely okay. [reassuring] And remember yourself: eat, drink and sit down if you need to. You help no one by fainting, and there's no shame in needing a chair.
17: [warmly] Whatever phase you're in, there are a few simple things that always help. Be present and put the phone down, it shows in the room when someone is really there. [pause] Breathe with her, one contraction at a time. Keep the practical things ready: water, a little snack and a cool towel. Use a calm voice, and pass her wishes on to the staff so she doesn't have to. [softly] And keep your own calm by eating, drinking and breathing yourself too. [reassuring] You don't need to do anything remarkable… your presence is what matters most.
18: [warmly] Sometimes a little help along the way is needed, and I want you to feel safe with that, because it's common and it's safe. [pause] It might be a drip to strengthen the contractions if they ease off, a ventouse for the last bit, or a caesarean, planned or emergency. The staff always explain what's happening and why, and you get to be involved the whole way. [reassuring] And this is important: another way into the world doesn't make the birth any less yours, and no less beautiful. [softly] The goal is a safe baby and a safe mother, whatever the path looks like.
19: [warmly] A practical tip: pack the bag in good time, ideally now, so it just stands ready by the door and you don't have to think about it later. [pause] For the mother: comfortable clothes, a nursing top, slippers, toiletries and a charger with a long cord, the cord is worth its weight in gold. For the baby: bodysuits, sleepsuits, a hat and a blanket, and make sure the car seat is ready in the car in good time. [softly] And for the partner: a change of clothes, food and drink, a card, and patience. [reassuring] Having this sorted takes away one worry, so you can be present in the moment itself.
20: [warmly] The first hours afterwards are quiet and enormous at the same time: skin to skin and the first breastfeed. [pause] The mother gets to rest, eat and pass urine, and that last one actually matters, so don't be surprised if we remind you about it. The baby is examined calmly, and you get time together before you move to the postnatal ward or head home. [softly] This can feel unreal, emotional and exhausting, all at once, and maybe the tears will come without you knowing why. [reassuring] That's completely normal. You've just been through one of the biggest things there is.
21: [warmly] You can do this. Trust your body, it knows what it's doing… [softly] trust each other, and the people around you. [reassuring] And carry this with you: you don't need to do it perfectly, you just need to do it together. If you have questions, just ask them.
